meta.custom: Fa'a Samoa places immense importance on the extended family and chiefs.
Tivaevae, hand-sewn patchwork quilts, are made as ceremonial gifts.
The Kava ceremony, an earthy infusion from a coconut shell, centers traditional welcomes.
The Heiva i Tahiti is a massive annual dance and sporting festival.
The Chenchule' is a Chamorro system of mutual gifts and assistance.
The Maneaba, a traditional meeting house, anchors village life.
Kemem celebrates a child's first birthday, historically a major survival milestone.
On Yap, giant stone discs called Rai are traditionally used as currency.
Weight lifting is a revered national sport tied to traditional strength.
The Kanak custom of 'La Coutume' involves offering yams or fabric to hosts.
Tāoga Niue preserves the island's language and traditional fishing ethos.
Local fiestas honoring patron saints feature abundant Chamorro food.
The Bai, a men's meeting house, was traditionally built without nails.
The Huli Wigmen grow their hair for months to craft ceremonial wigs.
Culture is tied to the Bounty mutineers' legacy and the Pitkern language.
Pe'a is the traditional male tattoo covering waist to knees.
Woven shell money is still used for traditional settlements and bride prices.
The Inati system divides all resources and fish catches equally among residents.
Wearing a ta'ovala, a woven mat, around the waist shows respect in formal settings.
The Fatele dance is performed to the beat of a wooden box on festive occasions.
Land diving (Naghol) on Pentecost Island preceded modern bungee jumping.
Traditional royalty remains influential, with ceremonial feasts and Kava central to island life.
